activities_projects.title: Testing Gustatory Receptors
activities_projects.objective: Understand how we detect taste through tongue receptors
materials: Bitters (like neem or bitter gourd juice)
steps: Rinse your mouth with water before starting.
Place a small amount of sugar on the tip of your tongue and identify the taste.
Repeat the process with salt, lemon, and bitter juice on different parts of the tongue.
Note which areas are most sensitive to each taste.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies that gustatory receptors are responsible for detecting chemical signals of taste.
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct mapping of the four basic tastes on a tongue diagram (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Smell and Taste Correlation
activities_projects.objective: Observe how olfactory receptors influence our perception of taste
steps: Blindfold a volunteer and ask them to close their nose tightly.
Give them a piece of apple to eat and ask them to identify it.
Now ask them to open their nose and eat another piece of the same fruit.
Compare the ease of identification and richness of flavor in both cases.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes that the brain combines signals from both gustatory and olfactory receptors to perceive flavor.
activities_projects.evaluation: Describing the difference in taste perception when the nose is blocked (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Reflex Action Simulation
activities_projects.objective: Observe an involuntary and near-instantaneous movement
materials: A small rubber mallet or the edge of a hand
steps: Sit on a chair with one leg crossed over the other so that the top foot hangs freely.
Strike the area just below the kneecap (patella) sharply with the side of your hand.
Observe the sudden upward kick of the lower leg.
Try to stop the movement consciously and see if you can.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies the 'Knee-jerk' reflex as an involuntary response controlled by the spinal cord.
activities_projects.evaluation: Explanation of why the leg moves before the brain consciously 'thinks' about it (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Phototropism in Plants
activities_projects.objective: Observe the movement of a plant towards light
materials: A cardboard box with a small hole on one side
steps: Place the plant inside the cardboard box.
Ensure the only source of light for the plant is the small hole on the side.
Keep the setup in a sunny area for 3-4 days.
Open the box and observe the direction in which the stem has grown.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Concludes that plant stems show positive phototropism by growing towards light.
activities_projects.evaluation: Sketch of the plant's growth direction relative to the light hole (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Geotropism Observation
activities_projects.objective: Observe the response of roots and shoots to gravity
materials: Support to keep the pot horizontal
steps: Place a healthy potted plant horizontally on its side.
Ensure the plant stays in this position for a few days.
Observe the direction of growth of the new shoot (stem) and the roots.
Note which part grows 'up' and which part grows 'down'.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that shoots show negative geotropism while roots show positive geotropism.
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct labeling of the plant parts as positively or negatively geotropic (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Nastic Movements: Mimosa pudica
activities_projects.objective: Observe a non-directional response to touch
steps: Touch the leaves of the Mimosa pudica plant gently.
Observe how quickly the leaflets fold up and the leaf droops.
Measure the time it takes for the leaves to return to their normal position.
Discuss if this movement involves growth or just changes in water amount (turgor).
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Differentiates between growth-related tropic movements and immediate nastic movements.
activities_projects.evaluation: Description of the 'drooping' response and the stimulus involved (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Human Brain Model
activities_projects.objective: Identify the major regions of the brain and their functions
steps: Use different colors of clay to model the Forebrain, Midbrain, and Hindbrain.
Label the Cerebrum, Cerebellum, and Medulla.
Write one primary function for each of these three parts (e.g., Cerebellum for balance).
Point out the location of the Hypothalamus.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Visualizes the structural division of the brain and assigns specific tasks to each region.
activities_projects.evaluation: Matching the brain part to its correct function (e.g., Medulla - Heartbeat) (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Hydrotropism Experiment
activities_projects.objective: Observe root growth in response to water
materials: A small porous clay pot (filled with water)
steps: Plant seeds in the tray of soil.
Bury the small clay pot in one corner of the tray and keep it filled with water.
Do not water the rest of the soil.
After a week, gently dig up the seedlings and observe the direction of root growth.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Demonstrates that roots grow towards the source of moisture (positive hydrotropism).
activities_projects.evaluation: Comparison of root direction in moist soil vs. dry soil (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Endocrine Glands Mapping
activities_projects.objective: Locate the major endocrine glands in the human body
steps: Mark the positions of the Pituitary, Thyroid, Adrenal, and Pancreas on the outline.
List the hormone secreted by each of these glands.
Write one change that occurs in the body due to Adrenaline (the 'emergency' hormone).
Identify which gland is known as the 'Master Gland'.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Associates specific glands with the hormones they release and their general impact on the body.
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct mapping of at least four glands and their hormones (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The 'Fight or Flight' Response
activities_projects.objective: Identify physical changes during a sudden scare
steps: Recall a situation where you were suddenly scared or surprised.
List the physical changes you felt (e.g., heart racing, sweating, rapid breathing).
Research which hormone was responsible for these changes.
Explain how these changes help the body prepare for action.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes the role of the endocrine system (Adrenaline) in providing immediate energy and alertness.
activities_projects.evaluation: Listing three physical responses to adrenaline and their benefit to the body (10 marks)
